title: Flight
description: Flight — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "Flight" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: Table of Contents
memes.description: Table of Contents — Flight by Walter White

memes.label: Ambition and Aspiration
memes.description: "The role of ambition, social climbing, and the American Dream in Flight — from Mary Robertson's determination to rise to Mimi's drive for economic independence"

memes.label: The Daquins' Arrival in Atlanta
memes.description: The Daquin family's arrival at Union Station in Atlanta, their first encounter with the city's segregated geography, and their introduction to the social world of Auburn Avenue.

memes.label: The Atlanta Constitution
memes.description: The white Atlanta newspaper whose inflammatory coverage of alleged assaults helped trigger the 1906 Atlanta Race Riot.

memes.label: The Atlanta Constitution
memes.description: "The white Atlanta newspaper whose sensational coverage helped fuel the 1906 race riot"

memes.label: Mimi's Flight from Atlanta to Philadelphia
memes.description: Mimi Daquin's solitary flight from Atlanta after discovering Carl Hunter's betrayal, boarding a train for an unknown city to begin a new life alone.

memes.label: Atlanta, Georgia
memes.description: "The primary setting of Flight — a booming New South city where the Daquin family relocates and where Mimi's racial consciousness is forged"

memes.label: Atlanta Police Department
memes.description: "The city police force whose complicity in the 1906 race riot illustrates the role of state violence in maintaining Jim Crow"

memes.label: Atlanta Race Riot of 1906
memes.description: The four-day racial massacre in Atlanta in September 1906 that drives the Daquin family from the city and shapes Mimi Daquin's racial consciousness for the rest of her life.

memes.label: Atlanta Union Station
memes.description: The Atlanta train station where the novel opens, marking the Daquin family's arrival from New Orleans and the threshold between the Creole world they left behind and the Jim Crow South they must now navigate

memes.label: Auburn Avenue, Atlanta
memes.description: 'The historic Black commercial district in Atlanta — known as the richest Negro street in the world — home to the business elite depicted in Flight'

memes.label: Auburn Avenue Business District
memes.description: "The thriving Black commercial district — the richest Negro street in the world — where Atlanta's Black elite built their fortunes"

memes.label: Aunt Sophie
memes.description: Mimi Daquin's aunt in New York, a widow who runs a beauty parlor and provides crucial refuge and emotional support throughout Mimi's adult life.

memes.label: Baltimore
memes.description: The city where Mimi places her son Jean in a Catholic orphanage, marking her lowest point before her journey to New York and a new life

memes.label: Bank
memes.description: "Financial institutions in the novel — sites of Black economic aspiration and white economic power, marking the boundaries of racial opportunity in early 20th-century America"

memes.label: Wheat Street Baptist Church
memes.description: "Representative of the Black Baptist churches that formed the institutional backbone of Atlanta's Black community"

memes.label: Bert Bellamy
memes.description: Wealthy Chicago-born bachelor and stockyard heir, a fixture in Jimmie Forrester's social circle whose gossip and empty sophistication represent the world Mimi enters through passing.

memes.label: Black Business Enterprise
memes.description: 'The parallel economy of Black-owned businesses in the Jim Crow era — insurance companies, banks, and retail establishments that served communities excluded from white commerce'

memes.label: Broadway (New York City)
memes.description: Manhattan thoroughfare known for theater and entertainment, where Mimi Daquin opens a dress shop and observes the Broadway scene.

memes.label: Canal Street (New Orleans)
memes.description: Major New Orleans thoroughfare that served as a racial and social dividing line in early 20th-century Creole society.

memes.label: Carl Hunter
memes.description: Son of Mr. and Mrs. Hunter of the Fleur-de-Lis Club in Atlanta, who represents the younger generation's discontent with Black respectability politics.

memes.label: Mimi's First Meeting with Carl Hunter
memes.description: Mimi Daquin's cool first encounter with Carl Hunter at a party, an inauspicious beginning to the relationship that will eventually reshape her life.

memes.label: The Chicago Defender
memes.description: The influential Black newspaper that encouraged the Great Migration, providing African Americans with news, advocacy, and a vision of opportunity in the North.

memes.label: The Chicago Defender
memes.description: "The influential Black newspaper that encouraged the Great Migration through its coverage of northern opportunity"

memes.label: Chicago, Illinois
memes.description: 'The destination of the Great Migration and the home of Mary Robertson before her marriage — representing northern opportunity and its limits'

memes.label: Class and Color
memes.description: 'The intertwined hierarchies of social class and skin color within early 20th-century Black American communities as depicted in Flight'

memes.label: "Colophon"
memes.description: "Colophon — Flight by Walter White"

memes.label: Color Prejudice
memes.description: 'Prejudice based on skin color within and across racial groups, a central social dynamic in the world of Flight'

memes.label: Concept Index
memes.description: Complete index of all concept entries in the Flight wiki, organized alphabetically with quality tiers

memes.label: Coney Island
memes.description: The Brooklyn amusement destination where Mimi escapes her sewing routine on summer trips with her Aunt Sophie and Harlem friends

memes.label: Creole Identity
memes.description: 'The complex racial and cultural identity of Creole people in early 20th-century New Orleans, as embodied by the Daquin family in Flight'

memes.label: The Creole Quarter
memes.description: The historic New Orleans neighborhood where Mimi Daquin was raised, center of Creole culture and society, and the world her family is forced to abandon as the old order collapses

memes.label: Crisis & Opportunity Books
memes.description: The publisher that produced the transcription of Flight on which the Standard Ebooks edition is based, preserving an important work of African American literature.

memes.label: Cultural Assimilation
memes.description: "The process by which minority individuals adopt the cultural norms of the dominant society, explored in Flight through Mimi's passing and the Creole community's negotiation with American racial categories"

memes.label: Arrival of the Daquin Family in Atlanta
memes.description: "The novel's opening scene — the Daquin family arrives by train in Atlanta, beginning their new life in the segregated South"

memes.label: Death
memes.description: "A recurring force in the novel — death as a catalyst for character transformation, a measure of racial violence, and the shadow that shapes Mimi Daquin's choices"

memes.label: Decatur Street (Atlanta)
memes.description: Atlanta thoroughfare in a slum district, scene of the racial violence that forces the Daquin family to flee the South.

memes.label: Dolores d'Aubigny
memes.description: Aspiring Broadway actress and the mistress of Horace Crosby, whose affair symbolizes the empty diversions of New York society in the later chapters of Flight.

memes.label: Dr. Adams
memes.description: Black physician in Atlanta, father of Hilda Adams, who treats Jean Daquin during his fatal illness and later diagnoses Mimi's pregnancy.

memes.label: Dr. Newton
memes.description: Prominent Black physician and surgeon in Philadelphia who operates on Petit Jean during his critical intestinal illness in Chapter 14.

memes.label: Education and Racial Uplift
memes.description: "The role of education as a strategy for racial advancement in early 20th-century Black communities, as explored through Mimi Daquin's trade school training"

memes.label: Event Index
memes.description: Complete index of all event entries in the Flight wiki, organized alphabetically with quality tiers

memes.label: Family and Motherhood
memes.description: "The role of family structures and motherhood in shaping identity and social belonging in the world of Flight, from Creole traditions to the challenges of single motherhood"

memes.label: Fashion and Self-Presentation
memes.description: "The role of clothing, fashion, and self-presentation as markers of class, identity, and aspiration in the world of Flight — from Creole elegance to New York modistes"

memes.label: Fifth Avenue
memes.description: The iconic Manhattan avenue that serves as a boundary between Mimi's Harlem life and the white world of fashion, wealth, and passing

memes.label: Fleur-de-Lis Club
memes.description: The elite Black women's social club in Atlanta that polices respectability and color boundaries within the city's African American community.

memes.label: The Fleur-de-Lis Club Meeting
memes.description: "The social gathering at Mrs. Hunter's home where Mimi first encounters intraracial color politics"

memes.label: Meeting of the Fleur-de-Lis Club at Mrs. Hunter's Home
memes.description: The April afternoon gathering at Mrs. Hunter's home where Mimi Daquin observes Atlanta's Black elite, meets her lifelong friend Hilda Adams, and witnesses the community's cruelty toward those who transgress racial boundaries.

memes.label: "Flight (Title Page)"
memes.description: "Flight (Title Page) — Flight by Walter White"

memes.label: Mimi's Employment at Francine's
memes.description: Mimi Daquin's first day of work as a finisher at Francine's, the exclusive New York dress shop, marking the practical beginning of her life passing as white.

memes.label: Francine's
memes.description: The exclusive New York modiste where Mimi Daquin works as a finisher while passing for white, run by the formidable Madame Francine.

memes.label: Government of the French Republic
memes.description: "The French government — referenced indirectly through the novel's engagement with French culture and colonial racial policies"

memes.label: Friendship and Community
memes.description: "The role of friendship and community bonds in sustaining Black life in Flight — from adolescent friendships to the networks of mutual aid that structure the social world"

memes.label: Funeral of Jean Daquin
memes.description: "The death and funeral of Mimi's father — the loss of her primary protector and the dissolution of the family's Atlanta life"

memes.label: Gender and Race
memes.description: 'The intersection of gender and racial identity in early 20th-century America, as experienced by Mimi Daquin and the women of Flight'

memes.label: The Gendered Moral Economy of Flight
memes.description: In Flight, women's social standing is policed through gossip, scandal, and moral judgment — a gendered moral economy that operates across all communities including New Orleans Creole, Atlanta's Black elite, Harlem's society, and white New York.

memes.label: Georgia Piedmont Region
memes.description: "The rural Georgia landscape surrounding Atlanta — a world of cotton fields, small farms, and the legacy of slavery"

memes.label: Georgia Piedmont
memes.description: The rural Georgia region surrounding Atlanta, whose red hills and cotton fields represent the broader Southern landscape and the harsh realities of rural black life under Jim Crow

memes.label: Germantown (Philadelphia)
memes.description: Philadelphia neighborhood where Mimi Daquin works as a domestic servant, site of traumatic experiences that shape her resolve to leave service.

memes.label: Grand Opera House (Atlanta)
memes.description: Atlanta theater and opera venue where Mrs. Adams attempts to pass for white by sitting in the white section, a pivotal scene in the novel's exploration of racial passing.

memes.label: The Great Migration
memes.description: The mass movement of African Americans from the rural South to northern cities in the early 20th century that structures the novel's geographical trajectory and the lives of its characters.

memes.label: The Harlem Scandal
memes.description: "The community scandal over Mimi's relationship with a married man that destroys her reputation in Harlem"

memes.label: Harlem, New York
memes.description: The center of Black cultural and political life in early 20th-century America, where Mimi Daquin finds community and ultimately experiences the betrayal that drives her to pass as white.

memes.label: The Harlem Scandal
memes.description: The public scandal in Harlem that destroys Mimi Daquin's reputation after her relationship with a married man becomes known, driving her to abandon the Black community and pass as white.

memes.label: Health and Medicine
memes.description: "Medical care, health disparities, and the role of Black physicians in the world of Flight — from childbirth in public hospitals to the limited healthcare options available to Black communities"

memes.label: Henry Meekins
memes.description: Young professor and dinner guest at the Forrester home whose critique of Western industrialism and racial assumptions challenges both Jimmie Forrester and Mimi Daquin.

memes.label: Hilda Adams
memes.description: Daughter of Mrs. Adams and Mimi Daquin's first close friend in Atlanta, whose companionship provides Mimi's introduction to peer relationships.

memes.label: Mimi and Hilda's First Meeting
memes.description: The meeting at the Fleur-de-Lis Club party where Mimi Daquin and Hilda Adams form an instant, intense friendship that becomes the novel's most enduring relationship between equals.

memes.label: Friendship of Hilda Adams and Mimi Daquin
memes.description: "The intense friendship formed between Mimi and Hilda Adams at the Fleur-de-Lis Club meeting"

memes.label: Home
memes.description: "The domestic sphere in the novel — home as a site of racial belonging, gendered labor, and the conflict between private identity and public performance"

memes.label: Horace Crosby
memes.description: Wealthy Chicago businessman whose fortune from grain and stockyard speculation funds his wife Eulalia Crosby's lavish lifestyle; Jimmie Forrester's business associate.

memes.label: "Imprint"
memes.description: "Imprint — Flight by Walter White"

memes.label: Intraracial Conflict
memes.description: "Conflict and competition within Black communities, a recurring dynamic in Flight that drives Mimi's alienation from her own people"

memes.label: Ivy Street (Atlanta)
memes.description: Atlanta street in the slum district, mentioned alongside Decatur Street as part of the city's most impoverished area.

memes.label: Jean Daquin
memes.description: Mimi's father, a Creole tailor from New Orleans whose pride in his ancestry and philosophy that ability matters more than color shapes the novel's central themes.

memes.label: Funeral of Jean Daquin
memes.description: The death and funeral of Mimi's father Jean Daquin, which leaves her without her primary protector and alters her relationship with the Atlanta community.

memes.label: Jerry Reed
memes.description: "A married man in Harlem with whom Mimi has a scandalous relationship — the catalyst for her decision to pass"

memes.label: Jim Crow Era
memes.description: 'The system of legal racial segregation that defined the American South and structured the world of Flight'

memes.label: Jimmie Forrester
memes.description: New York stockbroker introduced to Mimi Daquin by Mrs. Crosby in Paris; becomes Mimi's persistent suitor and eventual husband.

memes.label: Joe
memes.description: "A young Black man in Philadelphia who courts Mimi — representing a path of respectable Black life that Mimi cannot take"

memes.label: Labor and Employment
memes.description: "The landscape of work, occupations, and economic survival for Black Americans in the world of Flight — from domestic service to Black enterprise"

memes.label: Lenox Avenue
memes.description: The main thoroughfare of Harlem, where Mimi Daquin first encounters the vibrancy of Black urban life in New York

memes.label: Lincoln Club (Atlanta)
memes.description: Atlanta social club for Black businessmen, presided over by Mr. Hunter and mockingly called "the eating brigade" by the Fleur-de-Lis Club members.

memes.label: Lincoln Mutual Life Insurance Company
memes.description: The Black-owned insurance company based in Atlanta where Jean Daquin works, representing economic self-sufficiency in the early 20th-century African American community.

memes.label: Lizzie Stone
memes.description: "A domestic worker in Atlanta — representing the working-class Black experience in contrast to the elite Fleur-de-Lis Club world"

memes.label: Louisiana Bayou
memes.description: The rural Louisiana waterways and wetlands beyond New Orleans, which represent in the novel both the world of Jean Daquin's beloved outdoor life and the broader Southern countryside from which the Great Migration drew its people

memes.label: Lower East Side
memes.description: The Manhattan immigrant neighborhood where Mimi Daquin rents a room after leaving Harlem, completing her physical and social separation from the colored community

memes.label: Lower Manhattan, New York
memes.description: "The neighborhood where Mimi rents a room while working at Francine's, living as a white woman"

memes.label: Madame Francine
memes.description: "The owner of Francine's dress shop — the high priestess of the temple of feminine adornment where Mimi works while passing"

memes.label: Manhattan Trade School
memes.description: The New York City vocational school where Mimi Daquin receives her sewing training, representing the industrial education model available to Black women in the early 20th century.

memes.label: Margot Daquin
memes.description: Mimi Daquin's Creole mother and Jean Daquin's beloved first wife, whose death when Mimi was nine profoundly shaped the Daquin family's trajectory.

memes.label: Marriage and Romantic Love
memes.description: "Marriage customs, romantic love, and their intersection with race and class in the world of Flight — from Creole marriages to cross-racial unions"

memes.label: Marriage of Mary Robertson and Jean Daquin
memes.description: "The cross-cultural marriage between Jean Daquin and Mary Robertson that creates the blended family at the novel's center"

memes.label: Jean Daquin
memes.description: Mimi's father, a Creole tailor from New Orleans whose pride in his ancestry and philosophy that ability matters more than color shapes the novel's central themes.

memes.label: Memory and the Past
memes.description: "The role of memory, nostalgia, and ancestral heritage in shaping identity in Flight — from Jean Daquin's Creole reminiscences to Mimi's reckoning with her past selves"

memes.label: Mimi Daquin
memes.description: Central protagonist of Flight, a Creole woman from New Orleans who passes for white in her journey from southern Black society to the white working world.

memes.label: Mimi Daquin's Decision to Pass for White
memes.description: "The pivotal moment when Mimi Daquin resolves to leave the Black community and live as a white woman"

memes.label: Mimi Daquin's Employment at Francine's
memes.description: "Mimi's successful application for work at Francine's — the beginning of her life passing for white in the New York fashion industry"

memes.label: Mimi Daquin's Geographic Arc and Identity Transformation
memes.description: Mimi Daquin's geographic trajectory — New Orleans to Atlanta to Philadelphia to Harlem to Manhattan to Westchester to Paris and back to Harlem — maps onto her identity transformations, with each city representing a distinct racial self.

memes.label: Mimi Daquin's Decision to Pass
memes.description: The pivotal moment when Mimi Daquin decides to cross the color line permanently, renouncing her Black identity after the Harlem scandal destroys her reputation.

memes.label: Miss Lawrence
memes.description: Black fitter who was forced out of Francine's dress shop and established a successful independent dressmaking business in Harlem.

memes.label: Mr. Hunter
memes.description: President of the Lincoln Club, husband of Mrs. Hunter — a hustling, jovial man who befriends Jean Daquin and helps arrange his funeral.

memes.label: Mr. Manning
memes.description: Elderly Philadelphia caterer who, with his wife, took Mimi Daquin into their home after the birth of Petit Jean.

memes.label: Mr. Robertson
memes.description: Mary Robertson Daquin's father, a Chicago businessman obsessed with money and politics, who arranges the Daquin family's relocation to Atlanta.

memes.label: Mr. Stewart
memes.description: White bank cashier in Atlanta whose tense conversation with Jean Daquin in Chapter 5 illuminates the racial dynamics and mob violence fears of the early twentieth-century South.

memes.label: Mrs. Adams
memes.description: Member of the Fleur-de-Lis Club who was ejected from a white theatre for passing as white, an incident that defines her place in Atlanta's Negro society.

memes.label: Ejection of Mrs. Adams from the Grand Opera House
memes.description: A dramatic early example of the consequences of crossing racial boundaries, when Mrs. Adams is ejected from a white Atlanta theater for attempting to pass for white.

memes.label: Mrs. Bennett
memes.description: Wealthy, difficult-to-please customer at Madame Francine's dress shop whose approval of Mimi Daquin's design validates her talent and launches her reputation.

memes.label: Mrs. Crosby
memes.description: Wealthy Chicago socialite who introduces Mimi Daquin to Jimmie Forrester during a Paris luncheon in Chapter 19.

memes.label: Mrs. Hunter
memes.description: Wife of the Fleur-de-Lis Club president and hostess of Atlanta's Negro elite society in Walter White's Flight.

memes.label: Mrs. Sophronisba King
memes.description: An Atlanta neighbor of the Daquin family, lean and acidulous, who with Mrs. Plummer forms a gossipy pair monitoring the moral conduct of Auburn Avenue.

memes.label: Mrs. Manning
memes.description: Elderly Philadelphia caterer who, with her husband, took Mimi Daquin into their home and provided shelter and maternal care after the birth of Petit Jean.

memes.label: Jean Daquin
memes.description: Mimi's father, a Creole tailor from New Orleans whose pride in his ancestry and philosophy that ability matters more than color shapes the novel's central themes.

memes.label: Mrs. Rogers
memes.description: "Jean Daquin's sister in Harlem — Mimi's aunt who provides refuge and represents the family bonds of the Great Migration"

memes.label: Mrs. Williams
memes.description: Mimi Daquin's landlady in Philadelphia who cared for Petit Jean during his intussusception crisis and summoned Dr. Newton, helping to save the child's life.

memes.label: Music
memes.description: "A central motif in the novel — music as a vehicle of Black cultural expression, as a bridge across racial boundaries, and as a portal into Mimi Daquin's inner emotional life"

memes.label: National Association for the Advancement of Colored People
memes.description: The leading national civil rights organization founded in 1909, whose legal and political fight against Jim Crow forms the backdrop of the novel's racial politics.

memes.label: National Urban League
memes.description: "The civil rights organization focused on economic opportunity for African Americans in northern cities"

memes.label: Mimi Daquin's Childhood in New Orleans
memes.description: "Mimi's formative years in Creole New Orleans — the foundation of her identity before the disruptions of Atlanta"

memes.label: The Daquin Family's Departure from New Orleans
memes.description: The Daquin family's relocation from New Orleans to Atlanta, marking the end of Jean's Creole world and the family's entry into the harsher racial order of the New South.

memes.label: New Orleans, Louisiana
memes.description: "Mimi Daquin's birthplace and childhood home — a city with a distinctive Creole culture and racial order that shaped her earliest identity"

memes.label: New South Ideology
memes.description: 'The post-Reconstruction vision of an industrialized, economically modernized South, and its complex relationship to racial hierarchy in Flight'

memes.label: New York City
memes.description: "The metropolis where Mimi Daquin ultimately finds both freedom and isolation, the city that enables her to pass for white and the arena of her greatest professional success"

memes.label: 110th Street
memes.description: The street that forms the southern boundary of Harlem and serves as a symbolic dividing line between Black Harlem and white Manhattan in the early twentieth century.

memes.label: Organization Index
memes.description: Complete index of all organization entries in the Flight wiki, organized alphabetically with quality tiers

memes.label: Paris, France
memes.description: The French capital that serves as the cultural ideal Francine's Dress Shop emulates, the destination of Mimi's buying trips, and a symbolic space of freedom beyond American racial categories

memes.label: Peachtree Street
memes.description: Atlanta's main commercial thoroughfare, the center of white Atlanta's business and civic life, and the street that marks the boundary between the white and colored worlds

memes.label: Person Index
memes.description: Complete index of all person entries in the Flight wiki, organized alphabetically with quality tiers

memes.label: Petit Jean
memes.description: "Mimi Daquin's infant son — the child whose existence anchors Mimi to Black identity even as she passes for white"

memes.label: The Birth of Petit Jean
memes.description: The birth of Mimi Daquin's son in Philadelphia, an event that transforms her from a disgraced fugitive into a mother with a purpose, giving her the strength to survive.

memes.label: Mimi's Departure from Philadelphia
memes.description: "Mimi's decision to leave Philadelphia for New York — the final migration that will lead to her passing"

memes.label: Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
memes.description: "The Pennsylvania city where Mimi Daquin settles after leaving Atlanta, attempting to live respectably as a colored woman before ultimately choosing to pass for white in New York"

memes.label: Place Index
memes.description: Complete index of all place entries in the Flight wiki, organized alphabetically with quality tiers

memes.label: Poverty and Economic Class
memes.description: "Economic hardship, class stratification, and the pursuit of wealth in the Black community of Flight — the tension between Creole disdain for commerce and the American gospel of financial success"

memes.label: Pryor Street
memes.description: An Atlanta street marking the boundary between the Black business district and the dangerous slums, and the site of a lynching witnessed by Mimi during the Atlanta race riot

memes.label: Racial Identity
memes.description: "The formation and transformation of racial identity in early 20th-century America, as explored through Mimi Daquin's journey from Creole pride to passing for white"

memes.label: Racial Passing
memes.description: 'The practice of a person classified as a member of one racial group being accepted as a member of another, as explored in Walter White's Flight.'

memes.label: Religious Life
memes.description: 'The role of religious institutions and spiritual life in early 20th-century Black communities as depicted in Flight'

memes.label: Respectability Politics
memes.description: 'The strategy of demonstrating moral rectitude and middle-class values as a means of countering racist stereotypes in early 20th-century Black communities'

memes.label: Royal United Order of Heavenly Reapers
memes.description: A fraternal organization referenced in the novel, representing the network of mutual aid societies that served the Black community in the early 20th century.

memes.label: Salvation Army, Atlanta Corps
memes.description: "The charitable organization that appears during the Atlanta Race Riot, providing aid to victims"

memes.label: Shame and Social Honor
memes.description: "The role of reputation, scandal, and social honor as mechanisms of community control in the world of Flight — how gossip polices behavior and shame structures social belonging"

memes.label: Standard Ebooks
memes.description: The volunteer-driven project that produced the ebook edition of Flight used as the source corpus for this wiki.

memes.label: Standard Ebooks
memes.description: "The volunteer-driven project that produced this ebook edition of Flight, building on public domain transcriptions"

memes.label: Sylvia Smith
memes.description: "Mimi's coworker at Francine's — a white woman who unknowingly works alongside a Black woman passing for white"

memes.label: The Great Migration
memes.description: 'The mass movement of African Americans from the rural South to northern cities in the early 20th century, as experienced by Mimi Daquin and her family'

memes.label: Passing as a Spectrum of Racial Strategies
memes.description: In Flight, passing is not a single act but a spectrum of racial strategies — from complete passing as white to strategic accommodation to Creole visible respectability.

memes.label: "Uncopyright"
memes.description: "Uncopyright — Flight by Walter White"

memes.label: Racial Violence and Trauma
memes.description: 'The psychological impact of racial violence — from the Atlanta Race Riot to daily humiliations — on the characters of Flight'

memes.label: Washington Square (New York City)
memes.description: Elegant residential square in Greenwich Village, New York City, where Mimi Daquin attends social gatherings and observes the cultural elite.

memes.label: Westchester County (New York)
memes.description: Suburban county north of New York City where Mimi Daquin places her son Jean for care with a foster family.

memes.label: World War I
memes.description: The global conflict that accelerates the Great Migration by creating labor shortages in northern industries, opening economic opportunities for African Americans including Mimi Daquin.

memes.label: World War I in Flight
memes.description: "The global conflict that forms the historical backdrop to the novel's later chapters and shapes the world Mimi navigates"

memes.label: Wu Hseh-Chuan
memes.description: Chinese diplomat and intellectual whose observation that Black Americans "have successfully resisted mechanization" serves as a pivotal catalyst for Mimi Daquin's racial reawakening in the novel's final chapters.
